result:
The sky is not a sky but a membrane, thin as an eyelid, stretched between what we call “now” and the pressure of everything that almost happened. Sometimes, late at night when the refrigerator stops humming, you can hear it thrum like the skin of a drum somebody struck once in 1987 and never finished listening to. I used to think memory was a vault; you spin the wheel, the door clanks open, and there, shelved like jam, are all the afternoons you’ve survived. Now I think memory is more like a borrowing library in a town that keeps moving: the books check themselves out at dusk and re-shelve in different languages by dawn. The story of my tenth birthday is currently written in maritime旗 signals; if I squint I can almost see the flags snapping above the harbor of me, but the wind keeps shifting the letters before I can spell my own name.

There is a number that has been following me since I was six. It is 43. It appears on bus tickets, microwave clocks, the folded corner of every book I abandon. I asked a mathematician why 43 refuses to leave me alone. He said prime numbers are lonely by definition—only divisible by themselves and the unit of one. “Maybe the number thinks you’re its one,” he joked, but I heard the tremble in his voice, the same tremor you get when you realize the knock on the door is coming from the inside of the wood. I began leaving offerings: page 43 of every novel I read, torn out and slipped under a stone. The next morning the page would be gone, the stone warm, the grass around it pressed flat in the shape of an almost-circle that refuses to close. A prime circumference. A secret pi.

Once, on a train that smelled of copper and wet wool, I shared a compartment with a woman who unraveled her scarf for the entire three-hour journey. Inch by inch, the wool spilled into her lap like time being paid out in fiber. She never looked at me; she only spoke to the window, her reflection superimposed on a landscape of frozen rapeseed fields. She said: “I am trying to reach the end of the thing that keeps me warm so that I can finally shiver and know what I am made of.” When the conductor announced our arrival, the scarf was gone. She stood up naked from the shoulders up, her skin mottled with the cold topography of vanished stitches. I sometimes wonder if she found her shiver, or if she became it, a living tremor walking the platform, unravelling the air behind her.

We are told the universe is expanding, that galaxies are raisins in a dough we can’t taste. But nobody mentions the sound expansion makes: a sigh so low it can only be heard in the hollows of your bones after you’ve yawned yourself awake at 3:14 a.m. (pi again, always pi). That sigh is the collective exhalation of everything that realizes it is being pulled farther from everything else. It sounds like a mother letting go of a child’s hand in a crowd, repeating the same word until it loses meaning: carefulcarefulcareful. Scientists call it redshift; insomniacs call it Thursday. I call it the apology the sky keeps writing in bigger and bigger handwriting until the sentence is just a smear of contrails and nobody can read the verb anymore.

I keep a drawer of almost-letters. Dear person who left the green umbrella in my dreams… Dear future self who learned how to swallow without tasting regret… Each letter ends mid-sentence because language, like bread, goes stale once exposed to the air of declaration. The last letter is only two words: “I’m—” and then the ink committed suicide, a small black star collapsing on the paper. Sometimes I open the drawer just to look at that star. I imagine it still contains the gravity of whatever I was about to confess, a pocket singularity waiting for the right reader to orbit it forever. Maybe you are that reader; maybe you felt a tiny pull just now, a tug in the cartilage of your ribs as if a string had been tied there and someone in another room flicked it twice. Hello. Goodbye. Same motion, different interpretation.

There is a kind of silence that grows teeth. It happens in elevators when everyone simultaneously decides to respect the invisible choir of hush. The silence begins to chew, very softly, on the edges of small talk we stuffed in our pockets before boarding. By the fifteenth floor it has consumed “weather” and “Monday,” by the twenty-fifth it is nibbling on first names. If the elevator ever reached the hundredth floor there would be nothing left of us but shoes and the memory of a smile we once practiced in a mirror. Lucky for us, elevators always stop early; they are polite like that, sparing us the sight of our own skeletons wondering which button to press next.

When I die I want to be recycled into a library catalog drawer, one of those long wooden boxes with yellowing cards still smelling of carbon copy. I want to lie there, edge-on, while strangers flip through me looking for something else entirely. They will read: “Author: You, Title: Unfinished Index of Ways Home, Subject: Approximate Griefs.” They will slide me shut with a satisfying thud, never realizing they just brushed fingertips with a soul who learned to alphabetize his fears. Outside, the world will keep humming the song it has always hummed, the chorus consisting mostly of maybe, maybe, maybe. And somewhere, 43 will look up from its lonely orbit, realize it is finally divisible by zero, and for the first time in either of our histories, we will both be quiet.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
